era_context: The Napoleonic Wars are at their height in Europe. Napoleon has invaded Russia in June 1812 with the Grande Armée, beginning the disastrous Russian campaign that will lead to his eventual defeat. The War of 1812 between the United States and Britain has just begun. Latin American independence movements are accelerating. The Industrial Revolution is transforming Britain. The Qing Dynasty in China and the Tokugawa shogunate in Japan remain dominant in their regions.

events.title: War of 1812 begins; United States declares war on Britain
events.region: North America (United States and British North America)
events.description: The United States declared war on Britain on June 18, 1812, beginning the War of 1812. The war was fought over several issues: the impressment of American sailors by the British Navy, British support for Native American resistance to American expansion, and American desires to expand into Canada. The war lasted until December 1814 (Treaty of Ghent). The war was largely a stalemate, with neither side achieving its primary objectives. However, the war boosted American nationalism and confidence and led to the eventual defeat of the Indian Confederacy. The war also demonstrated American naval capability, particularly in naval battles on the Great Lakes.

sources.name: U.S. Congressional records and declaration of war
Hickey (1989), 'The War of 1812: A Forgotten Conflict'

events.certainty_note: The declaration of war is precisely dated (June 18, 1812) from Congressional records.

events.title: Napoleonic Wars: French invasion of Russia (Operation Barbarossa equivalent)
events.description: Napoleon invaded Russia on June 24, 1812, with the Grande Armée, consisting of approximately 600,000 soldiers (the largest army ever assembled at that time). The invasion was motivated by Napoleon's desire to defeat Russia and to enforce the Continental System (the blockade of Britain). The Russian campaign was disastrous for the French. The Russians, under General Mikhail Kutuzov, adopted a strategy of retreat and scorched earth, avoiding direct confrontation with the French army. The French captured Moscow on September 14, 1812, but found the city largely abandoned and burned. Unable to obtain supplies and facing the onset of winter, Napoleon was forced to retreat. The retreat was catastrophic, with the Grande Armée suffering approximately 400,000 casualties (killed, wounded, or captured). The Russian campaign marked the beginning of the end of Napoleon's dominance in Europe.

sources.name: Military records and eyewitness accounts from the campaign

events.certainty_note: The invasion date (June 24, 1812) and the capture of Moscow (September 14, 1812) are precisely documented in military records.

events.title: Battle of Borodino; French and Russian armies clash near Moscow
events.description: The Battle of Borodino was fought on September 7, 1812, between the French army under Napoleon and the Russian army under General Mikhail Kutuzov. The battle was one of the largest and bloodiest battles of the Napoleonic Wars, with approximately 70,000 casualties on both sides combined. The battle was tactically inconclusive, but the Russians were forced to retreat, allowing the French to advance toward Moscow. However, the Russian army remained intact and continued to resist. The battle demonstrated the fighting capability of the Russian army and the difficulty of defeating Russia. The battle is often seen as a moral victory for the Russians, as they prevented the French from achieving a decisive victory.

sources.name: Military records and eyewitness accounts from the battle

events.certainty_note: The battle date (September 7, 1812) is precisely documented in military records.

events.title: Peninsular War: Battle of Salamanca; British and Spanish defeat French
events.description: The Battle of Salamanca was fought on July 22, 1812, between the British and Spanish forces under the Duke of Wellington and the French forces under Marshal Auguste Marmont. The British-Spanish victory was decisive, with French casualties estimated at 7,000 killed, wounded, or captured. The victory allowed the British and Spanish to advance into central Spain and temporarily liberate Madrid. The battle was a major turning point in the Peninsular War and demonstrated the growing strength of the British-Spanish alliance.

sources.name: Military reports and eyewitness accounts from the battle
Oman (1930), 'A History of the Peninsular War'

events.certainty_note: The battle date (July 22, 1812) is precisely documented in military records.

events.title: Latin American independence movements accelerate; Venezuela and Colombia declare independence
events.description: The independence movements in Spanish America accelerated in 1812. Venezuela declared independence on July 5, 1812, establishing the First Venezuelan Republic. However, Spanish forces quickly suppressed the independence movement, and Venezuela was reconquered by 1812. Colombia (then New Granada) also experienced independence movements in 1812. The independence movements were driven by creole elites, mestizo soldiers, and indigenous populations seeking to escape Spanish colonial rule. The Spanish monarchy, weakened by the Peninsular War and the Napoleonic Wars, was unable to effectively suppress the independence movements.

sources.name: Spanish colonial records and independence movement documents

events.certainty_note: The declaration of Venezuelan independence on July 5, 1812, is documented in independence movement records.

events.title: Tokugawa shogunate issues edicts restricting foreign trade
events.description: The Tokugawa shogunate, under Shogun Tokugawa Ieyoshi, issued edicts in 1812 further restricting foreign trade and reinforcing the sakoku (closed country) policy. The edicts prohibited the construction of large ocean-going vessels and restricted foreign contact to the Dutch trading post at Nagasaki. This was part of the broader Tokugawa policy of isolationism that had been in place since the 17th century.

sources.name: Tokugawa bakufu edicts and administrative records

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Jiaqing Emperor consolidates power
events.description: The Jiaqing Emperor (r. 1796–1820) continued consolidating power in 1812, having suppressed the White Lotus Rebellion (1796–1804) and purged corrupt officials. The Qing Dynasty remained the dominant power in East Asia, but signs of decline were emerging: population pressure, agricultural stress, and the beginning of Western commercial pressure.

disconfirming_evidence: The War of 1812 is sometimes portrayed as an American victory, but modern scholarship has noted that it was largely a stalemate and that the United States failed to achieve its primary objectives (the conquest of Canada and the suppression of Native American resistance). The Battle of Borodino is often depicted as a French victory, but modern scholarship has emphasized that it was tactically inconclusive and that the Russians achieved a moral victory by preventing a decisive French victory. Some historians have questioned the extent to which the Russian campaign was truly 'disastrous' for the French, noting that the Grande Armée remained a formidable force even after the retreat.
historiographic_note: 1812 is exceptionally well-documented for Europe and North America due to rich archival records, military accounts, and diplomatic correspondence. The Napoleonic Wars and the War of 1812 are among the most thoroughly documented events of the period. However, sources for Latin America and other regions may be less complete in Western archives. The historical record for 1812 is among the richest for any year in this period.

graph_edges.note: French invasion of Russia in 1812 led to the collapse of French power and the Battle of Leipzig in 1813
Battle of Borodino was part of the French invasion of Russia
Battle of Salamanca in 1812 contributed to the British-Spanish victory at Vitoria in 1813
War of 1812 led to American victories at Lake Erie and the Thames in 1813
Latin American independence movements in 1812 accelerated in 1813 with Bolívar's campaigns
